segments.paradiso-11-g64: "Questa" is a feminine singular demonstrative pronoun (nominative) meaning "this one / she." The comma after it sets it off as the sentence's topic/subject; the remainder of the clause supplies qualifiers and actions referring back to this pronoun.
segments.paradiso-11-g65: "privata" is the past participle used adjectivally (feminine singular) from privare, meaning "deprived (of)," and it agrees with "Questa." "del primo marito" is a prepositional phrase (del = di + il) meaning "of the first husband," with "primo" = "first" and "marito" = "husband."
segments.paradiso-11-g66: This is a temporal-duration phrase + descriptive predicate leading to the verb: "millecent' anni e più" = "one thousand one hundred years and more" (apostrophe marks an archaic contraction of millecento). "dispetta e scura" are two adjectives (feminine) describing her state. "fino a costui" = "until this man" (fino a = up to). "si stette" is the passato remoto of stare with the reflexive "si," meaning "she remained/stayed," and "sanza invito" (archaic "sanza" = modern "senza") means "without invitation / without being invited."
segments.paradiso-11-g67: "né valse" = "nor did it avail" (valse = passato remoto of valere), with the infinitive "udir" (to hear) as its complement: "nor did it avail to hear that..." "che" introduces the subordinate clause; "la trovò sicura" = "he found her safe," where "la" is the direct object pronoun before the verb and "trovò" is passato remoto of trovare. "con Amiclate" is a prepositional phrase; here "Amiclate" is archaic/uncertain in force (likely an adjective or state meaning 'in friendship' or a proper name/person).
segments.paradiso-11-g68: "al suon de la sua voce" is a locative/causal prepositional phrase: "al" = a + il "at the," "suon" = "sound," "de" (archaic for di) = "of," and "la sua voce" = "her (or his) voice" (possessive preceded by the article, standard in Italian). Together: "at the sound of her voice."
segments.paradiso-11-g69: "colui" is a demonstrative pronoun meaning "that one / he who" and begins a relative clause introduced by "ch'" (= che, "who"). "a tutto 'l mondo" (a + tutto + il mondo) is an adverbial phrase meaning "throughout the whole world" or "to the whole world." "fé paura" uses passato remoto "fe' / fé" (old spelling of fece) from fare with "paura" as its object—literally "made fear" = "struck fear." The clause means "he who struck fear in the whole world."
segments.paradiso-11-g70: Another negative result clause: "né valse esser costante né feroce" = "nor did it avail to be constant nor fierce." "valse" is again passato remoto of valere; "esser" is the infinitive "to be" taking the predicate adjectives "costante" and "feroce." The double "né...né" corresponds to English "neither...nor."
segments.paradiso-11-g71: "sì che" here is a result conjunction meaning "so that" (sì = so; che = that), introducing the consequence clause that follows. It links the previous idea (that nothing availed) to the resulting fact.
segments.paradiso-11-g72: "dove Maria rimase giuso" is a relative/locative clause: "dove" = "where," "Maria" is the proper name and subject, "rimase" is passato remoto of rimanere meaning "remained," and "giuso" (archaic/poetic) = "below / down." Literally: "where Mary remained below."
segments.paradiso-11-g73: "ella con Cristo pianse in su la croce" is the main clause of the result: "ella" (stressed subject pronoun "she"), "con Cristo" = "with Christ," "pianse" = passato remoto of piangere "she wept," and "in su la croce" is a poetic/archaic locative prepositional phrase (= "upon the cross"); "in su" reinforces the sense "on/upon."
segments.paradiso-11-g74: Ma = 'but'. Perch’ is a contracted form of perché; here it introduces a purpose/negative-prevention clause ('so that...not') and is followed by the present subjunctive io non proceda (proceda = 1st person singular present subjunctive of procedere) used to express the speaker's wish/guard against proceeding in a certain way (equivalent to 'lest I proceed' or 'so that I do not proceed'). Troppo modifies the adjective chiuso; chiuso (literally 'closed') is used adjectivally/adverbially to mean 'narrowly' or 'too concisely' in this context.
segments.paradiso-11-g75: Prendi is the 2nd-person singular imperative of prendere ('take' / 'consider'), an address to the reader/listener. The phrase Francesco e Povertà are the items to be taken/considered; per questi amanti uses per in the sense 'as / for' (take X for Y = consider X to be Y). Oramai is an adverb 'now/at this time'. Nel mio parlar diffuso is a prepositional phrase modifying prendi: parlar is the infinitive used as a noun ('speech, telling') and diffuso is a postpositive adjective meaning 'wide-spread' or 'extended'.
segments.paradiso-11-g76: La lor uses the archaic possessive lor ('their') with the definite article la before the feminine noun concordia; lor has no vowel-ending here because of older/poetic usage. I lor lieti sembianti repeats lor + a plural noun: i (definite article) + lor (their) + lieti (plural masculine adjective) + sembianti (plural noun 'countenances/appearances') — agreement is normal (lieti/sembianti plural).
segments.paradiso-11-g77: The three nouns (amore e maraviglia e dolce sguardo) form the compound subject of facieno. Facieno is an archaic 3rd-person plural form (Dante's spelling) corresponding to modern fanno; here it functions as a narrative/present-tense 'make/cause'. The verb takes the infinitive esser ('to be') in the periphrastic expression facieno esser cagion = 'they made to be the cause' (i.e. 'they caused/produced'). Cagion di pensier santi = 'cause of holy thoughts' (di marks the relationship).
segments.paradiso-11-g78: Tanto che introduces a result clause ('so much that'). ’l is the elided form of il before a vowel (the apostrophe marks elision). Si scalzò is reflexive (si) + scalzò (passato remoto, 3rd sing. of scalzare) meaning 'he took off his shoes' or 'unshod himself'; prima here is 'first' ('first of all').
segments.paradiso-11-g79: E dietro a tanta pace: dietro a means 'after' or 'following' (literally 'behind') and introduces the circumstance; corse is passato remoto (3rd sing. of correre) meaning 'he ran' or 'he hurried', continuing the narrative action. The final e links to the participial clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-11-g80: Correndo is the gerund/present participle of correre ('running') used adverbially to indicate manner or simultaneous action ('as he ran' / 'running'). In Italian narrative it often modifies the main verb (corse) to describe how the running was done.
segments.paradiso-11-g81: Li is an older clitic dative pronoun placed before the verb (here meaning 'to him' or 'to them' in context). Parve is passato remoto of parere, 'seemed'; parve esser tardo is an impersonal/construction with parere + infinitive ('it seemed to him to be slow/late'). Tardo is an adjective meaning 'slow' or 'late' (here 'not quick enough' / 'too slow').
segments.paradiso-11-g82: Exclamatory Oh introduces an apostrophic exclamation. Ignota is the feminine adjective 'unknown' (agreeing with ricchezza) and ricchezza is a feminine noun 'wealth/richness'; the absence of a definite article and the adjective before the noun gives an emphatic, exclamatory force.
segments.paradiso-11-g1: This is a vocative phrase: 'O' calls out to the addressed thing; 'insensata' is an adjective modifying 'cura' and here means 'foolish' or 'senseless'; 'de' is the older form of 'dei' / 'degli' meaning 'of'; 'mortali' is a plural noun 'mortals' (i.e., human beings). Word order (vocative + adjective + noun + of-phrase) is poetic but normal for addressing an abstract idea.
segments.paradiso-11-g2: 'Quanto' introduces an exclamation 'how'; 'son' is the contracted present of 'sono' (they are); 'difettivi silogismi' = 'defective syllogisms' (an attributive adjective followed by noun); 'quei che' = 'those that/those who' begins a relative clause; 'ti fanno' = 'make you' (present tense, 3rd pl.); 'in basso batter l’ali' is an idiomatic phrase 'beat your wings low' where 'in basso' modifies the verb phrase, 'batter' is infinitive used after 'fanno', and 'l’ali' is the object 'the wings'. The clause describes cause: those bad arguments cause you to beat your wings down.
segments.paradiso-11-g3: 'Chi ... e chi ...' is a distributive pattern meaning 'some ... and some ...'; 'dietro a iura' literally 'after/behind the laws' = 'following the law(s)'; 'ad amforismi' = 'to/after aphorisms' = following aphorisms; 'sen giva' is an archaic sequence: 'se n'andava' contracted, where 'sen' = 'se ne' and 'giva' is the imperfect 'andava' (he/they used to go), so the clause says these people used to go/about in that direction.
segments.paradiso-11-g4: Another item in the enumeration: 'e chi seguendo sacerdozio' uses a gerund 'seguendo' ('pursuing' or 'following') with 'sacerdozio' (priesthood) to mean 'some who pursued the priesthood.' 'Chi' again is 'who/some who.'
segments.paradiso-11-g5: 'e chi regnar per forza o per sofismi' continues the list; 'regnar' is the infinitive 'to reign' used generically ('some who reigned'), 'per forza' = 'by force', 'per sofismi' = 'by sophisms/specious reasoning'—'per' marks the means.
segments.paradiso-11-g6: Parallel structure: 'e chi rubare' ('and some to steal' / 'some who stole') and 'e chi civil negozio' ('and some [to/for] civil business'). Dante uses infinitives and nouns in parallel to list typical human occupations or vices.
segments.paradiso-11-g7: 'chi nel diletto de la carne involto s’affaticava' — 'some, wrapped up in the delight of the flesh, labored' where 'involto' is a past participle meaning 'entangled/involved in'; 's’affaticava' is imperfect reflexive 'he/they fatigued themselves' = 'labored/struggled'; 'e chi si dava a l’ozio' = 'and some gave themselves to idleness' ('si dava' = 'devoted themselves to').
segments.paradiso-11-g8: 'quando' here introduces the temporal clause that follows: 'when' or 'at which time'.
segments.paradiso-11-g9: 'da tutte queste cose sciolto' is an ablative/agent-like phrase meaning 'having been freed/loosened from all these things'; 'sciolto' is a past participle used adjectivally to mean 'released' or 'free'.
segments.paradiso-11-g10: This is the main result clause: 'con Bëatrice m’era suso in cielo cotanto glorïosamente accolto.' The clitic+verb 'm’era' = 'mi era' (to me was / I had been) functions with the past participle 'accolto' to form a compound meaning 'I was received'; 'suso in cielo' = 'up into heaven'; word order places the manner adverbial 'cotanto gloriosamente' before the participle for emphasis. Note the older placement of the clitic before the auxiliary.
segments.paradiso-11-g11: 'Poi che' = 'after/when'; 'ciascuno fu tornato' uses the passato remoto/perfect idea ('each one had returned' or 'each returned') with 'fu' + past participle 'tornato'; 'ne lo punto del cerchio' = archaic form of 'nel punto del cerchio' ('to/in the point of the circle'); 'in che avanti s’era' = 'in which before he had been' where 's’era' = 'si era' (he had been). The clause frames a completed past action setting the scene.
segments.paradiso-11-g12: 'Fermossi' is an archaic perfect form of the reflexive verb 'fermarsi' (to stop/stand still); the clitic is enclitic and the whole is a single preterite/perfect verb meaning 'they/each one stopped.'
segments.paradiso-11-g13: 'come a candellier candelo' is a simile: 'like a candle to a candlestick.' 'Come a' introduces the comparison; the noun order is poetic (object after its support) and implies 'each light stood as a candle on its candlestick.'
segments.paradiso-11-g14: 'E io senti’ dentro a quella lumera' — 'and I heard/ perceived inside that lamp/light': 'senti’' is the contracted past historic 'sentii' (I heard or I perceived); 'dentro a quella lumera' = 'within that light'; 'che pria m’avea parlato' is a relative clause 'which before had spoken to me' where 'm’avea' = 'mi aveva' (had to me / had spoken to me).
segments.paradiso-11-g15: 'sorridendo incominciar' — the gerund 'sorridendo' ('smiling') expresses the manner of the ensuing action; 'incominciar' is an infinitive used to mean 'to begin' (Dante often uses the infinitive in narrative contexts for immediacy). Together they mean 'it began, smiling' or 'it began to smile.'
segments.paradiso-11-g16: 'faccendosi più mera' — 'faccendosi' is a reflexive gerund ('making/setting itself' or 'becoming'); 'più' modifies the adjective 'mera' ('more mere/ more pure/ more simple' depending on nuance). The phrase means roughly 'becoming purer/simpler.'
segments.paradiso-11-g17: The reported speech begins: 'Così com’ io del suo raggio resplendo,' — 'just as I shine by/through his ray.' 'Così com’' = 'just as' and 'resplendo' is present indicative 'I shine,' giving a general or immediate truth within the speech.
segments.paradiso-11-g18: 'sì' is a resumptive adverb 'so' linking the simile to what follows ('so,' or 'thus').
segments.paradiso-11-g19: 'riguardando ne la luce etterna' uses the present participle 'riguardando' ('looking back into' / 'considering'); 'ne la' = 'in the' (archaic 'in' usage), so the phrase means 'by looking into the eternal light' or 'when one looks into the eternal light.'
segments.paradiso-11-g20: 'li tuoi pensieri' = 'your thoughts' (li = the definite article 'the'); 'onde cagioni apprendo' — 'onde' = 'from which' or 'by which,' 'cagioni' = 'causes/reasons,' and 'apprendo' is present indicative 'I learn' or 'I perceive' (Dante uses it to mean 'I learn/ascertain' here). The whole clause: 'from which I learn the causes of your thoughts.'
segments.paradiso-11-g21: Tu is the vocative/subject 'you'. Dubbi is an older poetic 2nd-person present meaning 'you doubt' (an archaic/elliptical verbal form here), so the phrase means simply 'You doubt.'
segments.paradiso-11-g22: This clause uses the periphrastic expression 'hai voler' (hai + voler) = 'you have a wish / you want' followed by a subordinate clause introduced by che. 'Si ricerna' is an impersonal/reflexive subjunctive (si + ricerna) meaning 'be sifted/clearly distinguished'; 'in sì aperta e ’n sì distesa lingua' is an adverbial phrase 'in such an open and so wide language' (’n is a contracted/inverted form of in), and 'lo dicer mio' is the direct object 'my saying/what I say' (lo + dicer + mio).
segments.paradiso-11-g23: Ch’ is the contracted che 'that'. 'Al tuo sentir' is a dative/locative phrase 'to/at your hearing' where sentir functions as a noun ('hearing, perception'). 'Si sterna' is reflexive/passive (si + sterna) of sternare: here it means 'is laid low/overwhelmed' or 'is made to lie low' — a present/subjunctive-like sense matching the subordinate clause introduced by che.
segments.paradiso-11-g24: Ove = 'where' or 'in that place'; dinanzi = 'before / previously / earlier'; dissi is the 1st-person singular passato remoto 'I said'. Together this points back to something the speaker said earlier: 'where previously I said.'
segments.paradiso-11-g25: Quoted line. U’ (u) = 'wherein / where'; ben = 'good' (here a substantive sense, 'goodness'); s’ impingua is reflexive (s’ = si) + impingua (subjunctive/indicative form of impingua­re) meaning 'becomes enriched' or 'is made rich' — literally 'where good is enriched.'
segments.paradiso-11-g26: e = 'and'; là = 'there' (pointing to another place previously mentioned); u = 'where'; dissi = 'I said' (passato remoto). This introduces the second place previously mentioned.
segments.paradiso-11-g27: Quoted sentence. Non = 'not'; nacque is passato remoto of nascere 'was born' or 'arose'; il secondo = 'the second' (the second one). Literally: 'The second was not born.'
segments.paradiso-11-g28: e qui = 'and here / at this point'; è uopo = impersonal phrase 'it is necessary' (è + noun uopo); che + verb (distingua) introduces a subjunctive subordinate: 'that it be well distinguished.' ben = 'well' (adverb); si distingua uses reflexive si in a passive/impersonal subjunctive sense.
segments.paradiso-11-g29: La provedenza = 'Providence' with the definite article; the noun is the subject of the following relative clause. The phrase names Divine Providence as the actor.
segments.paradiso-11-g30: Relative clause introduced by che 'which/that': governa il mondo = 'governs the world' (present indicative). 'Con quel consiglio' = 'with that counsel/plan'; 'nel quale' = 'in which' introduces a subordinate clause where 'ogne aspetto creato è vinto' = 'every created aspect is overcome' (passive: è vinto). 'Pria che vada al fondo' = 'before it goes to the bottom/depth' (pria che + subjunctive vada gives a temporal clause 'before it goes').
segments.paradiso-11-g31: 'Però che' here introduces the purpose/result clause: 'so that...' Andasse is imperfect subjunctive of andare ('might go / should go'); ver’ is a contracted poetic form of verso 'toward' (here 'toward his delight'); 'la sposa di colui' = 'the spouse/bride of him' (colui = 'he who'); 'ch’ ad alte grida disposò lei col sangue benedetto' is a relative clause 'who with loud cries appointed her with blessed blood' — disposò is passato remoto 'he appointed/ordained.'
segments.paradiso-11-g32: in sé sicura = 'secure/confident in herself' (in + reflexive sé + adjective); 'e anche a lui più fida' = 'and also more trusting/faithful toward him' (a lui = to him, più fida = comparative adjective 'more faithful'). The construction balances self-assurance and trust toward him.
segments.paradiso-11-g33: Due principi ordinò = 'she (Providence) ordained/appointed two princes' (ordinò = passato remoto 'ordained/ordered'). 'In suo favore' = 'in her favour' (in + possessive suo + favore). Note: principi here means 'princes/leaders' in context.
segments.paradiso-11-g34: che introduces a purpose/result clause: 'that from here and from there they might be for her as guides.' Quinci e quindi = 'on this side and on that side / from here and from there'; le is the feminine object/pronoun 'her'; fosser is an archaic subjunctive (fossero) used after che to express potentiality/role; per guida = 'as a guide/for guidance.'
segments.paradiso-11-g35: L’un = 'the one' (the article + uno) identifying one of the two; fu is passato remoto 'was'; tutto here is an intensifier 'entirely/wholly'; serafico in ardore = 'seraphic in ardour' (serafico = seraphic, in ardore = 'in fervour/ardour').
segments.paradiso-11-g36: l’altro = 'the other'; per sapïenza = 'by/for wisdom' (per indicating the quality/reason); in terra fue = 'on earth he was' (fue is an archaic past form 'was'); di cherubica luce uno splendore = inverted apposition 'a splendour of cherubic light' (di + light + uno splendore). The word order is poetic/inverted: the predicate 'uno splendore' follows the qualifying phrase.
segments.paradiso-11-g37: De l’un = 'of the one' (about the one); dirò is future 'I will say / I will speak' — the speaker announces he will describe one of them.
segments.paradiso-11-g38: però che = 'because' or 'for the reason that'; d’amendue = 'of both' (di + amendue 'both'); si dice l’un pregiando = an impersonal/passive construction 'one speaks of the one when praising' (si dice = 'it is said' / 'one says', l’un = 'the one', pregiando = present participle 'praising').
segments.paradiso-11-g39: qual ch’om prende = 'whichever one a man takes' (qual = 'which/whatever', ch’ = chi 'who/that', om = 'man/one', prende = present 'takes'). It expresses 'whichever of the two a person prefers.'
segments.paradiso-11-g40: perch’ = 'because/for that reason'; ad un fine = 'to one end / for one purpose'; fur is archaic passato remoto plural of essere used here ('they were'); l’opere sue = 'his works' (l’ + opere + sue). The clause says the works of the two tended to the same single end.
segments.paradiso-11-g83: An exclamatory interjection phrase. "oh" is a poetic vocative/exclamation placed before the adjective; "ben" is an adverb (here meaning "well/richly") modifying the adjective "ferace"; "ferace" is an adjectival, somewhat archaic word meaning "fertile/productive." Word order is emphatic: interjection + adverb + adjective.
segments.paradiso-11-g84: "Scalzasi" is the verb scalzarsi with the enclitic reflexive "-si" attached; here it is 3rd-person singular present (poetic usage) meaning "becomes unshod / goes barefoot." The name "Egidio" follows the verb by inversion for a terse, poetic effect.
segments.paradiso-11-g85: The same reflexive verb "scalzasi" applies to the following person. "Silvestro" (the person named) is the logical subject (here listed after the verb). "dietro a lo sposo" is a prepositional phrase: "dietro a" = "after/behind" and "lo sposo" = "the bridegroom." The phrase means he goes barefoot following the bridegroom; word order (verb — subject — prepositional phrase) is a poetic inversion.
segments.paradiso-11-g86: "sì" here is an adverb meaning "thus/so" (not merely "yes"); it modifies the whole clause that follows. "la sposa" is the noun phrase "the bride/the wife" (subject), and "piace" is 3rd-person singular present of piacere used intransitively here = "is pleasing" / "pleases." The verb construction in Italian places the pleasing experiencer differently than in English; Dante states simply that the bride pleases (is pleased/pleasing).
segments.paradiso-11-g87: "Indi" = "then/after that." "sen va" is a contracted/separable form of "se ne va" (se + ne + va) meaning "goes away/goes on his way;" the reflexive/partitive pronoun is cliticized. "quel padre e quel maestro" identifies the subject (the same person described as father and master). "con la sua donna e con quella famiglia" = "with his lady and with that household." The relative clause "che già legava l'umile capestro" modifies "famiglia": "that the humble capestro (halter/noose) already bound." "legava" is the imperfect tense used narratively to indicate a continuing/previously established binding.
segments.paradiso-11-g88: "Né" is the negative conjunction "nor/neither." "li" is an old clitic (here functioning as a pronoun referring back to him/them) attached before the verb in object position. "gravò" is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of "gravare," meaning "weighed down/pressed upon." The nominative cause "viltà di cuor" = "cowardice of heart" is the thing that weighed upon "le ciglia" (the eyelids/eyes); "per esser fi’ di Pietro Bernardone" is a purpose/reason phrase "for being a son of Pietro Bernardone," where "fi’" is an abbreviated/poetic contraction for "figlio" (son).
segments.paradiso-11-g89: "né" continues the negation: "nor." "per parer" uses the infinitive "parer(e)" (to seem/appear) to indicate motive or intended appearance. "dispetto" = "spite/insult/displeasure" and "a maraviglia" = "to/for wonder" or "to provoke astonishment." The whole phrase means "nor (did he act) to seem spiteful or to provoke astonishment."
segments.paradiso-11-g90: "ma" = "but/rather." "regalmente" is an adverb "regally/majestically," modifying the ensuing action. "sua dura intenzione" = "his stern/firm intention" (possessive + adjective + noun). "ad Innocenzio" is the indirect object "to Innocent (the pope)." "aperse" is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of "aperire/aperse" (poetic/archaic form) meaning "opened/revealed"—he revealed his intention to the pope.
segments.paradiso-11-g91: "e da lui ebbe" = "and from him he received/obtained;" "da lui" is the source/dative. "ebbe" is passato remoto of "avere" used here as "received/obtained." "primo sigillo" = "the first seal" (a formal papal endorsement), and "a sua religïone" means "for his religion/order"—in medieval usage "religione" often denotes a religious order or way of life. The sequence states that the pope gave the first formal seal to his order.
segments.paradiso-11-g41: Intra is a preposition meaning 'between' (poetic/archaic variant of modern fra/tra) and introduces the two geographic elements joined by e. l’ (il) is the elided definite article attached to acqua. che is the relative pronoun 'which/that' referring back to acqua, and discende is the present indicative 'flows down' (discendere) describing the water’s motion. del colle eletto dal beato Ubaldo is a full prepositional phrase: del = di + il 'of the', colle = 'hill', eletto is a past-participle adjective 'chosen' modifying colle, and dal = da + il 'by the' introduces the agent (the blessed Ubaldo) who chose the hill.
segments.paradiso-11-g42: Fertile modifies costa and stands before the noun in typical Italian adjective–noun order for emphasis. d’alto monte is a reduced genitive/partitive: di + alto monte = 'of/from a high mountain.' Pende is the present indicative of pendere and here means 'slopes down' or 'hangs down' — a stative/actional verb describing the slope’s position.
segments.paradiso-11-g43: Onde introduces a consequence or origin 'from which/whence' linking the previous clause to Perugia’s climate. Sente is present indicative of sentire 'feels', here taking direct objects freddo e caldo ('cold and heat') to express that Perugia experiences both. da + place (Porta Sole) marks the source 'from Porta Sole' (Porta Sole is a gate/place-name).
segments.paradiso-11-g44: E links this clause to the prior one. di rietro = 'from behind' (di + rietro, an adverbial phrase). Le is a feminine plural/clitic form referring back to Perugia (here used like 'it/them' in older usage). Piange is present indicative 'weeps' or 'laments'. Per introduces the cause/reason 'because of' or 'by reason of' and modifies piange; grave giogo = 'heavy yoke' (giogo = yoke/ridge/burden), and the final Nocera con Gualdo names the places doing the lamenting or lying behind (they are grammatically the subject of piange in the sense 'Nocera with Gualdo laments it').
segments.paradiso-11-g45: Di here introduces source/partitive 'from/of' (Di questa costa = 'from this slope'). Questa is the demonstrative adjective 'this', and costa is the feminine noun 'slope' (here referring back to the slope mentioned previously).
segments.paradiso-11-g46: Là dov’ is a fixed locative 'there where'. Ella is the feminine pronoun 'it/she' referring to the slope; frange is present indicative of frangere 'breaks' (poetic present describing where the slope breaks/dashes), and più sua rattezza literally 'more its swiftness' = 'most of its swiftness' (più modifies the following possessive + noun and indicates degree). The unusual word rattezza means 'swiftness/rapidity'.
segments.paradiso-11-g47: Nacque is the passato remoto (past historic) of nascere 'was born' or 'arose'; al mondo = 'to/into the world' (al = a + il) and un sole = 'a sun' (sole). The construction means a sun 'appeared' there in the world — a poetic way to say a bright sun-like sight arose.
segments.paradiso-11-g48: Come introduces a simile 'as'. Fa is present of fare 'does' (here 'happens/occurs'); questo refers back to the sun/phenomenon just described; talvolta = 'sometimes'; di Gange = 'of the Ganges' (locative: occurring sometimes around the Ganges). The whole clause says the same phenomenon sometimes occurs on the Ganges.
segments.paradiso-11-g49: Però = 'therefore' or 'however' introducing the remark. Chi = 'who/ whoever' is the indefinite relative. d’esso loco = 'of that place' (d’ = di + esso 'that/it', loco = 'place'). Fa parole literally 'makes words' — an idiom meaning 'speaks' about it (fa + noun used as verbal expression).
segments.paradiso-11-g50: Non dica is present subjunctive (dica) with non to give an admonition or polite prohibition: 'let him not say/call'. Dante uses the subjunctive here where modern English uses an imperative or modal. Ascesi is the name/term the speaker warns against using (referring to a traditional name).
segments.paradiso-11-g51: Ché = 'for'/'because' giving the reason; direbbe is the conditional 'would say/call' (dire in conditional mood) describing the result of using Ascesi; corto = 'short'/'insufficient' meaning 'imprecise' or 'too narrow' (i.e., the name would fall short of the full meaning).
segments.paradiso-11-g52: Ma = 'but/rather' introducing the preferable term. Orïente (with diaeresis marking vowel break) is a substantive meaning 'the East' or 'Orient' — Dante recommends this name instead.
segments.paradiso-11-g53: Se ... vuole is a conditional clause 'if (he) wishes'; se = 'if', proprio = 'precisely/properly' (here 'if he wants to be precise'), dir is the infinitive 'to say', and vuole is present 'wants'. The inversion 'dir vuole' is a compact poetic word order (infinitive before finite verb).
segments.paradiso-11-g54: Non era ancor is the negative imperfect 'was not yet' (era = imperfect of essere). Molto modifies lontan 'very far', and da l’orto = 'from the garden' (da + the elided article). The whole phrase sets the temporal/locational point: 'it was not yet far from the garden.'
segments.paradiso-11-g55: Ch’ is the conjunction 'that/when' introducing the following clause. El is an archaic third-person pronoun (egli/esso) 'it/he' referring to the thing just mentioned; cominciò is passato remoto 'began', a + infinitive (a far sentir) marks the start of an action 'to make [something] feel'. Far sentir = causative 'to make feel'; la terra = 'the land' (object), de la sua gran virtute = 'of its great virtue/power' (virtute here means effective power/efficacy), and alcun conforto = 'some comfort/consolation' (alcun as an indefinite quantifier).
segments.paradiso-11-g56: Ché = 'for/because'. Per tal donna = 'for/through such a woman' (per introduces motive or agency); tal is 'such' and donna here is best read as a courtly 'lady' (in Dante often the beloved lady).
segments.paradiso-11-g57: Giovinetto is an appositive noun 'young man' referring to the male protagonist; it stands alone as an explanatory tag.
segments.paradiso-11-g58: In guerra is an idiomatic phrase 'into conflict' or 'in a war/struggle'; del padre = 'of/with his father' (del = di + il), and corse is the passato remoto of correre 'ran' used here to mean 'rushed' or 'took action' — a compressed narrative past describing that the youth took up arms (or opposed) against his father.
segments.paradiso-11-g59: A cui is a prepositional relative 'to whom' or 'to which' pointing forward to the father (introduces the following remark about how the father treated him).
segments.paradiso-11-g60: Come a la morte = 'as to death' (come = 'as/like', a la = 'to the', morte = 'death'); the simile compares the father's attitude toward pleasure with how one treats death (i.e., the gate of pleasure is closed as if facing death).
segments.paradiso-11-g61: La porta del piacer = 'the gate of pleasure' (la porta + del = of the + piacer = pleasure). Nessun is the negative quantifier 'no one/none' and diserra is a present-tense (in context, generalizing) or archaic form meaning 'unbolts/loosens' (literally 'does not open' here: 'no one unbolts the gate of pleasure' — i.e., he denies access).
segments.paradiso-11-g62: E = 'and'. Dinanzi a = 'before/in the presence of'. La sua spirital corte = 'his/her spiritual court' (sua = possessive; spirital = 'spiritual'; corte here functions as 'court' or 'assembly' — poetic vocabulary), et coram patre = Latin phrase 'and in the presence of the father' (coram = 'before', patre = 'father'). Le si fece unito is a reflexive/impersonal construction: le (to her) + si (reflexive) + fece (past historic of fare 'made/caused') + unito (united) — 'he made himself united to her' (i.e., they were joined/married in her spiritual court and before the father).
segments.paradiso-11-g63: Poscia = 'afterward/then'. Di dì in dì is an idiom meaning 'from day to day' or 'day by day' (the repetition expresses progressive increase). L’amò is passato remoto 'he loved her' (amò = amare), with the enclitic l’ = 'her' attached; più forte intensifies the verb 'loved more strongly/more deeply'.
segments.paradiso-11-g92: "Poi che" here functions as a subordinating conjunction meaning "after/when." The clause has the collective subject "la gente poverella" (a diminutive/affectionate form meaning the humble/poor folk) and the verb "crebbe" (passato remoto, 3rd person singular) meaning "grew"; "dietro a costui" = "behind this man."
segments.paradiso-11-g93: "la cui mirabil vita" = "whose wondrous life," with the relative pronoun "cui" (= "whose") modifying "vita." The phrase "meglio in gloria del ciel si canterebbe" uses the conditional "canterebbe" with reflexive/passive "si" to mean "would be sung better in the glory of heaven."
segments.paradiso-11-g94: This is an inverted passive clause. The participial phrase "di seconda corona redimita" (past participle "redimita" agreeing in gender with the feminine noun it will modify) precedes the auxiliary "fu" to form the passive "was redeemed." "Per Onorio" gives the human agent, and "da l'Etterno Spiro" names the divine agent ("from/by the Eternal Breath/Spirit"). The final noun phrase is "la santa voglia d'esto archimandrita" (the holy desire of this archimandrite).
segments.paradiso-11-g95: "E poi che" again introduces the following narrative clause: "and then/after that." It is a short temporal/conjunctive phrase preparing the reason clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-11-g96: "per la sete del martiro" is a prepositional phrase of motive: "out of/for the thirst for martyrdom." "Sete" is the noun "thirst" and "del martiro" specifies that the thirst is for martyrdom.
segments.paradiso-11-g97: "ne la presenza del Soldan superba" = "in the proud presence of the Sultan," an adverbial locative phrase ("ne" = "in/within"). The main verb "predicò" (passato remoto, 3rd sing.) follows: "he preached Christ," and "e li altri che 'l seguiro" appends "and the others who followed him" ("seguiro" = archaic past form of "seguirono").
segments.paradiso-11-g98: This gives the reason and purpose: "e per trovare a conversione acerba troppo la gente" = literally "and (in) finding the people too harsh for conversion" (word order is inverted; "trovare ... la gente acerba" = "to find the people hard to convert"). "E per non stare indarno" = "and so as not to remain there in vain," with "per non + infinitive" expressing purpose.
segments.paradiso-11-g99: "redissi al frutto de l'italica erba" = "he returned to the fruit/produce of the Italian soil." "Redissi" is a past form meaning "returned"; "al frutto de l'italica erba" is a poetic expression for "to Italy" or to its produce/result.
segments.paradiso-11-g100: Locative phrase "nel crudo sasso intra Tevero e Arno" = "on/in the harsh rock between the Tiber and the Arno." "Da Cristo prese l'ultimo sigillo" uses "da" = "from" to mark the source/agent and the verb "prese" (passato remoto) meaning "he received/took" the "ultimo sigillo."
segments.paradiso-11-g101: Relative clause introduced by "che" referring back to "l'ultimo sigillo": "which his limbs bore for two years." "Portarno" is an archaic passato remoto (3rd person plural) meaning "they carried/bore"; here the subject is "le sue membra."
segments.paradiso-11-g102: Quando introduces a temporal clause ('when'). a colui ch’a tanto ben sortillo is an indirect-object phrase 'to him who had been allotted so much good' (sortillo is a diminutive/archaic noun meaning a small lot/fate). piacque is passato remoto of piacere used impersonally ('it pleased'), followed by di + infinitive (di trarlo suso = 'to draw/bring him up'); trarlo = trar + lo (clitic 'him'), and suso = 'up'. a la mercede is a goal 'to the reward'; ch'el meritò is a relative clause ('which he deserved') and nel suo farsi pusillo means 'in/for his making himself small' (farsi = reflexive 'to make oneself', pusillo = 'small/humble').
segments.paradiso-11-g103: a’ frati suoi: a (to) introduces the indirect object 'to his brothers'; frati is the plural 'brothers' (religious brothers/friars) and suoi is the possessive 'his', placed after the noun in the older/poetic order.
segments.paradiso-11-g104: sì com’ a giuste rede: sì com' (contracted sì come) = 'just as' or 'as'; a introduces the comparison/recipient 'to'; giuste rede = 'rightful heir(s)' (rede is an archaic noun 'heir', giuste = 'just/right'). The whole phrase qualifies the manner in which he entrusted her (as one would to a rightful heir).
segments.paradiso-11-g105: raccomandò la donna sua più cara: raccomandò is passato remoto 'he entrusted/commended'; la donna sua places the possessive after the noun (poetic order) meaning 'his lady' or 'his lady/thing to be cared for' (here 'donna' = beloved lady or figurative lady); più cara = 'most dear' (superlative).
segments.paradiso-11-g106: e comandò che l’amassero a fede: e = 'and'; comandò (passato remoto) = 'he commanded/ enjoined'; che introduces a subordinate clause; l’amassero is third-person plural imperfect subjunctive of amare used after a past-tense verb of command (a past-jussive sense: 'that they should love her'); a fede = 'in faith/faithfully', i.e. with fidelity.
segments.paradiso-11-g107: e del suo grembo l’anima preclara mover si volle: e = 'and'; del suo grembo = 'from/of his bosom/womb' (del = di + il); l’anima preclara = 'the illustrious/very clear soul' (preclara = illustrious/celebrated); mover si volle is an inversion of si mover volle (mover = infinitive 'to move/depart', si = reflexive/clitic, volle = passato remoto of volere 'wished'), literally 'wished to depart'.
segments.paradiso-11-g108: tornando al suo regno: tornando is the present participle 'returning' (expressing concomitant action or manner), al suo regno = 'to his kingdom' (a + il = al). The participial phrase explains where the soul goes.
segments.paradiso-11-g109: e al suo corpo non volle altra bara: e = 'and'; al suo corpo = 'for his body' (dative/benefactive); non volle (passato remoto) = 'he did not want/would have no'; altra bara = 'another coffin' (i.e. he refused any other burial chest). The negation non precedes the verb voler (wanted/refused).
segments.paradiso-11-g110: Pensa is a 2nd-person singular imperative (or hortatory present) meaning “consider” or “think.” Oramai is an adverb meaning “now/at this point.” Qual fu colui che is an expository relative phrase: qual (which/what) + fu (past tense of essere, 'was') + colui che ('the one who'), so “what/which was the one who…”; degno collega fu a mantener displays older word order: degno (worthy) modifies collega (colleague), and fu a + infinitive (a mantener) expresses the role or function — “served as a worthy colleague to maintain/uphold.” La barca di Pietro in alto mar are straightforward noun phrases (“Peter’s boat on the high sea”); per + noun (per dritto segno) indicates manner or means (“by/through a straight/right sign”).
segments.paradiso-11-g111: E is the simple conjunction “and” linking the clauses. Questo fu is a demonstrative + past-tense verb: “this was.” Il nostro patrïarca is the noun phrase “our patriarch”; the diaeresis on ï in patrïarca is an orthographic mark showing the syllable break (archaic/poetic spelling).
segments.paradiso-11-g112: Per che here functions as a connective meaning “therefore/for that reason” (an older/conjunctive use of per + che). Qual is an indefinite/relative pronoun meaning “whoever/one who,” and segue is present indicative (‘follows’) expressing a general truth; lui is the emphatic pronoun “him.”
segments.paradiso-11-g113: Com’ is an apocopated form of come (the apostrophe marks the omitted e) meaning “as” or “how.” El is an archaic subject pronoun (regional/poetic) equivalent to modern egli/egli = “he.” Comanda is present indicative “commands” or “directs.” The phrase com’ el comanda = “as he commands.”
segments.paradiso-11-g114: Discerner è l'infinito 'to discern' placed before the finite verb puoi — a poetic/archaic inversion (infinitive + finite verb) that yields the sense “you can discern.” Puoi is 2nd-person singular present of potere (‘you can’). Che introduces the subordinate clause “that ….” Buone merce carca is an inverted/elliptical phrase meaning “laden with good merchandise”: merce (merchandise) and carca (lit. ‘loaded/filled’) form a predicate phrase, with word order altered for metrical/poetic reasons.
segments.paradiso-11-g115: Ma = 'but'. '’l' is the elided article (il) before suo. 'suo pecuglio' literally 'his peculium/pecuglio' (a patrimony or private holding used metaphorically). 'di nova vivanda' = 'for new/fresh food' with di introducing the type/purpose of the pecuglio. 'è fatto ghiotto' is a resultative construction: è + past participle fatto ('has been made/has become') + adjective ghiotto ('greedy/gluttonous') describing the resulting state.
segments.paradiso-11-g116: sì ch’ = 'so that'. 'esser non puote' is an archaic periphrasis meaning 'it cannot be' (esser + puote, with puote = archaic form of potere). 'che' introduces the subordinate clause. The clause contains a double-negation construction common in medieval Italian: 'non puote ... non si spanda' (= 'it is not possible that ... not ...'), i.e. 'it cannot be that it does not spread'. 'si spanda' is reflexive/passive: 'spreads itself' or 'is scattered'.
segments.paradiso-11-g117: This is a comparative-correlative pattern: 'e quanto ... più ...' = 'and the more ... the more ...'. 'le sue pecore remote e vagabunde' = 'his sheep, distant and wandering', adjectives placed after the noun. 'più ... da esso vanno' = 'the more they go away from him' where 'vanno' is present indicative plural and 'da' marks motion away from 'esso'.
segments.paradiso-11-g118: 'più tornano' completes the comparative: 'the more ... the more they return'. 'a l’ovil' = 'to the sheepfold' (a + il elided as a l’); 'ovil' is the archaic form of ovile (sheepfold). 'di latte vòte' literally 'empty of milk' (di + thing they lack; vòte = archaic plural 'empty'), so the fold is 'empty of milk'.
segments.paradiso-11-g119: Ben = 'indeed' or 'well' used for emphasis. 'son' = 'are' (sono). 'di quelle che' = 'among those who' (i.e. 'there are those who'). 'temono ’l danno' = 'fear the harm/damage' with l' = lo elided. 'stringonsi al pastor' uses the reflexive verb stringersi ('they draw themselves close' i.e. 'they cling') and 'al' = a + il 'to the' plus pastor 'shepherd'.
segments.paradiso-11-g120: ma = 'but'. 'son' = 'are'. 'sì poche' = 'so few' (sì used adverbially for emphasis before the adjective). The whole short clause yields 'but they are so few'.
segments.paradiso-11-g121: che introduces the result clause 'that ...'. 'le cappe' = 'the cloaks' (subject); 'fornisce' is third-person singular present of fornire 'provides' (Dante sometimes allows unexpected agreement/number for metrical or syntactic reasons, but here the sense is 'provide'). 'poco panno' = 'little cloth' (poco modifying panno), so 'the cloaks supply little cloth'.
segments.paradiso-11-g122: Or = 'now' (an introductory particle signaling a new point).
segments.paradiso-11-g123: se = 'if'. 'le mie parole' = 'my words' (subject). 'non son fioche' = 'are not feeble/faint' (non + son = are not; fioche = archaic plural adjective 'feeble/weak').
segments.paradiso-11-g124: se = 'if'. 'la tua audïenza' = 'your hearing/attention' (note diaeresis marks pronunciation). 'è stata attenta' = perfect tense 'has been attentive' (è + past participle stata; attenta agrees with audïenza).
segments.paradiso-11-g125: se = 'if'. 'ciò ch’è detto' = 'that which is said' (ciò + che + è detto). 'a la mente revoche' = 'calls back to the mind': revoche is a present form (indicative/subjunctive shape in medieval spelling) of revocare meaning 'recall' or 'bring back' and takes 'a la mente' as its indirect object.
segments.paradiso-11-g126: in parte = 'in part'. 'fia' is an archaic/future form of essere here meaning 'will be' (3rd singular future). 'la tua voglia contenta' = 'your desire/wish (voglia) content/pleased' (contenta agrees with voglia). Together: 'in part your desire will be satisfied.'
segments.paradiso-11-g127: perché = 'because'. 'vedrai' = future 'you will see' (2nd person sing.). 'la pianta' = 'the root/base/source' (pianta used figuratively). 'onde' = 'from which/whence'. 'si scheggia' = reflexive/present 'it splinters/chips off' (si + scheggia).
segments.paradiso-11-g128: e = 'and'. 'vedra’' (vedrai/vedrà with elision) = 'you will see'. 'il corrègger' = 'the corrector/reformer' (a noun meaning one who corrects). 'che argomenta' = 'who argues/maintains' (argomenta present indicative). Then the direct quotation begins with the archaic 'U’' = 'where'. 'ben' = 'good'. 's’impingua' = 'fatten(s) itself' (si = reflexive/impersonal; impingua = present subjunctive/indicative of impinguare 'to fatten/enrich').
segments.paradiso-11-g129: se non si vaneggia = 'if one does not vaneggiare' (vaneggia = present 'rave/delude oneself/talk nonsense'); here si is impersonal/reflexive ('one' or 'itself'). The proverb completes the quotation: 'Where good fattens itself, if one does not rave.'

sentences.paradiso-11-s13: She, deprived of her first husband, remained for eleven hundred years and more in neglect and obscurity until this man — she stayed without being invited; nor did it avail to hear that he found her safe with Amiclate at the sound of her voice, that one who struck fear through the whole world; nor did it avail to be steadfast or fierce, so that where Mary had remained below, she with Christ wept upon the cross.
sentences.paradiso-11-s14: But so that I do not proceed too narrowly, take Francis and Poverty as these lovers now in my extended speech.
sentences.paradiso-11-s15: Their harmony and their cheerful countenances — love and wonder and a sweet glance — gave rise to holy thoughts; so much so that the venerable Bernard first took off his shoes, and, following such peace, ran on; and, running, it seemed to him that he was slow.
sentences.paradiso-11-s1: O foolish anxiety of mortal men, how defective the syllogisms are that make you beat your wings low!
sentences.paradiso-11-s2: Some followed the law and some aphorisms, some pursued the priesthood, some reigned by force or by sophisms, some stole and some attended to civil business, some, wrapped in the delight of the flesh, labored at it and some gave themselves to idleness; when, freed from all these things, with Beatrice I had risen up into heaven and was received there so gloriously.
sentences.paradiso-11-s3: After each had returned to the point of the circle where he had been before, they stopped, like a candle to its candlestick.
sentences.paradiso-11-s4: And I heard, from within that light which had spoken to me before, it begin smiling and, making itself more pure, say: "Just as I shine from his ray, so, looking into the eternal light, I learn the causes of your thoughts."
sentences.paradiso-11-s5: You doubt, and you wish that my saying be sifted (sorted out) in such an open and so wide tongue — that it be laid low at your hearing; previously I said in one place: “Where goodness is enriched,” and there I said: “The second was not born”; and here it is necessary that it be well distinguished.
sentences.paradiso-11-s6: Providence, which governs the world with that counsel in which every created aspect is overcome before it goes to the depths, so that the spouse of him — that he ordered with high cries, with blessed blood — might go toward his delight, secure in herself and even more trusting in him, ordained two princes in her favour, that from here and from there they might be for her guide.
sentences.paradiso-11-s7: One of them was wholly seraphic in ardour; the other, for wisdom on earth, was a splendour of cherubic light.
sentences.paradiso-11-s8: I will speak of the one, because when one praises them both one speaks of the one, whichever man chooses, since his works were for one purpose.
sentences.paradiso-11-s18: Egidio goes barefoot; Silvestro goes barefoot after the bridegroom; thus the bride is pleased.
sentences.paradiso-11-s19: Then that father and that master goes on his way with his lady and with that household which the humble halter had already bound.
sentences.paradiso-11-s20: Nor did cowardice of heart weigh down his eyelids for being a son of Pietro Bernardone, nor to seem, from spite, to cause wonder; but regally he opened his stern intention to Innocent, and from him he received the first seal for his order.
sentences.paradiso-11-s9: Between the Tupino and the water that flows down from the hill chosen by the blessed Ubaldo, a fertile slope of a high mountain hangs down, whence Perugia feels cold and heat from Porta Sole; and from behind Nocera with Gualdo laments it because of a heavy yoke.
sentences.paradiso-11-s10: From this slope, there where it breaks most of its swiftness, a sun was born into the world, as this sometimes happens on the Ganges.
sentences.paradiso-11-s11: Therefore whoever speaks of that place should not call it 'Ascesi', for he would be speaking narrowly, but call it Orient if he wishes to speak precisely.
sentences.paradiso-11-s12: It was not yet very far from the garden, when it began to make the land feel some comfort from its great power; for on account of such a lady the young man ran into conflict with his father, to whom, as to death, the gate of pleasure no one unbolts; and before her spiritual court and in the presence of the father he was united to her; afterward day by day he loved her more strongly.
sentences.paradiso-11-s21: After the humble band of poor people grew up behind this man, whose wondrous life would better be sung in the glory of heaven, the holy desire of this archimandrite was redeemed with a second crown by Honorius through the Eternal Breath.
sentences.paradiso-11-s22: And then, because of the thirst for martyrdom, he preached Christ — and the others who followed him — in the proud presence of the Sultan; and, finding the people too hard to convert and not wishing to remain there in vain, he returned to the fruit of the Italian soil; on the harsh rock between the Tiber and the Arno he received from Christ the last seal, which his limbs bore for two years.
sentences.paradiso-11-s23: When it pleased him — the one to whom so great a good had been allotted — to lift him up to the reward which he had deserved by making himself small, he entrusted his most beloved lady to his brothers, as to rightful heirs, and commanded that they love her faithfully; and the illustrious soul wished to move from his bosom, returning to its kingdom, and for his body he would have no other coffin.
sentences.paradiso-11-s24: Consider now who that was who proved a worthy colleague in upholding Peter's boat on the high sea by a straight sign; and this was our patriarch; therefore whoever follows him, as he commands, you can discern is laden with good merchandise.
sentences.paradiso-11-s25: But his peculium, greedy for fresh provender, has become greedy, so that it cannot be that, by various leaps, it does not scatter itself; and the farther his sheep — remote and wandering — go away from him, the more they return to the sheepfold emptied of milk.
sentences.paradiso-11-s26: There indeed are those who fear harm and cluster about the shepherd; but they are so few that the cloaks provide little cloth.
sentences.paradiso-11-s27: Now, if my words are not weak, if your hearing has been attentive, if what has been said calls back to the mind, in part your wish will be satisfied; because you will see the root whence it splinters, and you will see the corrector who argues, 'Where good fattens itself, if one does not rave.'
